# Preface {.unnumbered}

:::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
::: column-margin
![](cover.png)
:::

[Click here for a PDF version](Statistics-Minus-the-Math--2e-.pdf) of this book.
::::

***Note: This is an unstable version** that will be finalized and launched as version 2.1 in mid-July. For all major changes I plan to make, I have left notes at the beginning of relevant chapters indicating what will change prior to the mid-July launch. The most recent stable version (2.0) can be accessed here: <https://minusthemath.com/2e>.*

This textbook introduces contemporary uses of statistics in the social sciences, emphasizing foundational skills in data description, quantitative reasoning, and statistical inference that are increasingly important for students preparing to enter the workforce in our data-driven age.

Accessible yet rigorous explanations prioritize conceptual understanding over extended mathematical treatments. The practical focus equips students to quickly get started evaluating and producing simple statistical analyses. The relatively compact book is divided into two halves, with part one offering a crash course on the essentials of statistics-based analysis. Part two provides more in-depth coverage of fundamental principles in quantitative research, such as probabilistic modeling, causality, measurement, and emerging uses of artificial intelligence. The power of simple statistical techniques is demonstrated using examples from throughout the social sciences, including practical uses of data like those students are likely to encounter on the job. Explanations also highlight the importance of drawing on practical knowledge of the context surrounding the data in order to critically evaluate assumptions underlying measurement, visualization, and inference. Readers will realize how the reasoning skills they’ve used their entire lives can help them effectively describe, contextualize, and interpret patterns in data.

*Statistics Minus the Math* will appeal to undergraduates and early postgraduate students who are new to statistics—especially those who lack confidence in math. It can be used as a main text for introductory statistics, data analysis, or quantitative research methods courses. It also makes for a good supplementary textbook for courses with a broader focus on research methods, data science, or thesis writing.

## How this book is different

There several things that make this book is a bit different from most introductory statistics texts:

-   It emphasizes three topics that are rarely found together in one text: (1) data description, (2) quantitative reasoning, and (3) causality in social science research.

-   There are diverse social science examples, with a particular emphasis on organizational studies (especially public and nonprofit sectors) and public policy. These are practical and relatable areas of focus for many social science students.

-   Contemporary approaches to quantitative social science are prioritized, resulting in greater attention to regression modeling and confidence intervals.

-   The ordering of topics is designed to equip students to start working with data and reading quantitative social science literature as quickly as possible. The first three chapters not only teach data description and visualization but also provide basic guidelines for reading a regression results table.

-   Chapters are relatively short (usually around 6000 words), allowing for quick reading and making clear the most essential material for students to master on each topic. Given the broad audience of this text, it may be helpful to read these short chapters alongside applied examples from one's field of study. Chapter appendices sometimes provide additional content that may be important for more technically-oriented versions of an intro course.

-   Instructors may be interested in further considerations outlined in Teaching Resources section at the end of the book.

This digital book is meant to be a free resource, and this version is licensed under [CC BY-NC-ND 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/). The book was made using [Quarto](https://quarto.org/) and is hosted using [GitHub Pages](https://pages.github.com/).

This is still a work in progress. If you find errors, feel free to reach out (find updated contact info here: <https://nathanfavero.com>) so I can correct them for the next version I publish.

## Acknowledgements

Portions of this book (and most of the original version) are adapted from the public domain resource *Online Statistics Education: A Multimedia Course of Study* (<https://onlinestatbook.com> Project Leader: David M. Lane, Rice University). A huge thanks to David Lane and his colleagues at Rice University for their creation of this wonderful resource. I use footnotes throughout to indicate when specific sections are adapted from Lane and colleagues. Currently, @sec-describing-one-variable and @sec-sampling-distributions are mostly derived from this public domain resource, in addition to sections of @sec-qual-associations, @sec-h-tests, @sec-causality and @sec-regression.

I would also like to thank Natasha Kallish for help transforming an earlier version of this text from Word to a Quarto document.
